concepts.title: Hydrogenation of alkenes
concepts.conceptBody:
Preparation Of Alkanes

1. Hydrogenation of alkenes

Hydrogenation is the chemical reaction that results from the addition of hydrogen $\left( {{\text{H}}_{2}} \right)$. The process is usually employed to reduce or saturate organic compounds. The process typically constitutes the addition of pairs of hydrogen atoms to a molecule. Catalysts are required for the reaction to be usable; non-catalytic hydrogenation takes place only at very high temperature. Hydrogen adds to double and triple bonds in hydrocarbons.

  • Alkenes can be reduced to alkanes with ${{\text{H}}_{2}}$ in the presence of metal catalyst such as $\text{Pt},\text{Pd}$, Ni or Rh.
  • The two new C-H $\sigma $ bonds are formed simultaneously from $\text{H}$ atoms absorbed on to the metal surface.
  • The reaction is stereospecific giving only the ‘syn’ addition product .

Catalytic Hydrogenation

Note: Since hydrogenation is ‘syn’ addition, hence we can observe the following :

concepts.title: Wurtz reaction for alkanes
concepts.conceptBody:
Wurtz reaction

Wurtz reaction , is a coupling reaction whereby two alkyl halides are reacted with sodium to form a new carbon-carbon bond:

$2R-X+2Na\xrightarrow[ether]{\text{dry}}R-R+2NaX$

Note: Methane is not formed by this method.

Mechanism: 

This reaction involves the radical species $\stackrel{\bullet}{\text{R}}$

One electron from sodium is transferred to the halogen to produce a sodium halide and an alkyl radical.

$\text{R}-\text{X}+\text{Na}\to \stackrel{\bullet}{\text{R}} +\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}\text{X}$

The alkyl radical then accepts an electron from another sodium atom to form an alkyl anion and the sodium becomes cationic.

$\stackrel{\bullet}{\text{R}} +\text{Na}\to {{\text{R}}^{-}}\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}$

The alkyl anion then displaces the halide in an ${{\text{S}}_{\text{N}}}2$ reaction, forming a new carbon-carbon covalent bond.

$\text{R}-\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}+\text{RX}\to \text{R}-\text{R}+\text{N}{{\text{a}}^{+}}\text{X}$

Example

$2\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{I}+2\text{Na}\xrightarrow[\text{ether}]{\text{anhydrous}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+2\text{NaI}$

$2\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{Cl}+\underset{\text{ Sodium }}{\mathop{2\text{Na}}}\,\xrightarrow[\text{ether}]{\text{anhydrous}}\underset{\text{ n-butane }}{\mathop{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}}}\,+\underset{\text{ Sodium Chloride }}{\mathop{2\text{NaCl}}}\,$

Here methyl iodide and ethyl chloride are reacted with powered sodium metal in anhydrous ether. Alkanes containing double the number of carbon atoms are formed, i.e., methyl iodide gives ethane, and ethyl chloride gives $\text{n}$-butane.

The solvent, ether in this case, must be anhydrous (free of moisture) become the alkyl anions are so basic they readily deprotonate water to hydroxide ion, forming alcohols, and reducing the yield of the desired product.

Limitations

(1) The wurtz reaction is limited to the synthesis of symmetric alkanes. If two dissimilar alkyl halides are taken as reactants, then the product is a mixture of alkanes that is, often, difficult to separate.

(2) Tertiary alkyl halides do not give wurtz reaction. (Since it follows ${{\text{S}}_{\text{N}}}2$ mechanism)

Illustration

This is formed because C-Br bond is weaker than C-Cl bond.

concepts.title: Clemmenson and wolf kishner reduction
concepts.conceptBody:
Clemmensen reduction

The Clemmensen reduction is a chemical reaction described as a reduction of ketones (or aldehydes) to alkanes using zinc amalgam and hydrochloric acid.

The Clemmensen reduction is particularly effective at reducing aryl-alkyl ketones.

With aliphatic or acyclic ketones, zinc metal reduction is much more effective.

  • The substrate must be stable in the strongly acidic conditions of the Clemmensen reduction. As a result of Clemmensen Reduction, the carbon of the carbonyl group involved is converted from $\text{s}{{\text{p}}^{2}}$ hybridisation to $\text{s}{{\text{p}}^{3}}$ hybridisation. The oxygen atom is lost in the form of one molecule of water.

4. Wolff-Kishner reduction

The Wolff-Kishner reduction is a chemical reaction that fully reduces a ketone (or aldehyde) to an alkane.

Reaction mechanism

The most probable mechanism involves the elimination of an alkyl anion as the final step:

The mechanism first involves the formation of the hydrazone in a mechanism that is probably analogous to the formation of an imine. Successive deprotonations eventually result in the evolution of nitrogen. The mechanism can be justified by the evolution of nitrogen as the thermodynamic driving force.

  • Wolff-kishner reduction, utilizes strongly basic conditions.

concepts.title: Kolbe's electrolysis
concepts.conceptBody:
Kolbe electrolysis

The Kolbe reaction is formally a decarboxylative dimerisation and proceed by a radical reaction mechanism. Generally, the reaction can be observed as:

$2\text{RCO}{{\text{O}}^{-}}\to \text{R}-\text{R}+2\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

As an example, electrolysis of acetic acid yields ethane and carbon dioxide:

$\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{COOH}\to \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CO}{{\text{O}}^{-}}\to \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{CO}{{\text{O}}^{\bullet }}\to \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}^{\bullet }+\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}$

$2\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}^{\bullet }\to \text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$

A concentrated solution of sodium or potassium salt of a carboxylic acid or mixture of carboxylic acids is electrolysed.

$\text{RC}{{\text{O}}_{2}}~\text{K}+{{\text{R}}^{\prime }}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}~\text{K}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow[{}]{\text{Current}}\text{R}-{{\text{R}}^{\prime }}+2\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}+2\text{KOH}$

$2\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}~\text{K}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}\xrightarrow[{}]{Current}\underbrace{\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+2\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}}_{\text{anode }}+\underbrace{2\text{KOH}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}}_{cathode}$

  • If R and ${{\text{R}}^{\prime }}$ are different, then hydrocarbons $\text{R}-{{\text{R}}^{\prime }},\,\,\,\text{R}-\text{R},\,\,\,{{\text{R}}^{\prime }}-{{\text{R}}^{\prime }}$ are also obtained.
  • Esters, lower alkane and alkenes are also obtained as side products.

${{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}~\text{K}\rightleftharpoons {{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{CO}_{2}^{-}+{{\text{K}}^{+}}$

${{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{CO}_{2}^{-}-{{\text{e}}^{-}}\to {{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{CO}_{2}^{\bullet }\,(I)$

$\underset{(I)}{\mathop{{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{CO}_{2}^{\centerdot }}}\,+\underset{(II)}{\mathop{{{\text{C}}_{2}}\text{H}_{5}^{\bullet }}}\,\to \underset{\text{ester}}{\mathop{{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{C}{{\text{O}}_{2}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}}}\,$

${{\text{C}}_{2}}\text{H}_{5}^{\bullet }+{{\text{C}}_{2}}\text{H}_{5}^{\bullet }\xrightarrow[\text{Hydrogenation}]{\text{Intermolecular}}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{6}}+{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$

concepts.title: Using organometallic compounds
concepts.conceptBody:
Using orgno-metallic compounds

Organometallic compounds react as electron rich or anionic carbon atoms i.e. as carbanions, which means they will function as either base or nucleophiles.

It is reasonable to think of these organometallic compound as ${{\text{R}}^{-}}{{\text{M}}^{+}}$.

Basicity: 

The following equation represents the loss of a proton from a generic hydrocarbon forming a carbanion:

$\underset{\text{ hydrocarbon }}{\mathop{\text{R}-\text{H}}}\,\rightleftarrows \underset{\text{carbanion}}{\mathop{\text{R}\overset{-}{\mathop{\text{:}}}\,{{\text{H}}^{+}}}}\,$

  • Organolithium and organomagnesium compound are strong bases since the negative charge is on carbon.
  • Simple carbanions are strong bases, since the C is not very electronegative (compared to N or O)
  • In the presence of weak acid, RLi and RMgX protonate giving the hydrocarbon.

(a) Decomposition of Grignard Reagent

Grignard reagent (an organametallic compound) on treatment with water or dilute acid, or alcohol is decomposed to alkanes.

(R is alkyl or aryl X is Cl, Br, I)

It is the $\text{R}$ part of the Grignard Reagent that is converted into $\text{RH}$.

If heavy water ${{\text{D}}_{2}}\text{O}$ is used, then product is

$\text{RMgX}+\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\to \text{R}-\text{H}+\text{Mg}\left( \text{N}{{\text{H}}_{2}} \right)\text{X}$

$\text{RMgX}+\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{OH}\to \text{RH}+\text{Mg}\left( \text{OC}{{\text{H}}_{3}} \right)\text{X}$

(b) Coupling of alkyl halides with organometallic compounds corey–House Synthesis

For good yields $R'X$ should be a primary halide, the alkyl group $\text{R}$ in the organametallic may be primary, secondary or tertiary, for example.

NOTE : Unlike wurtz Reaction, this reaction can be employed to prepare unsymmetrical alkanes

8. Reduction by dissolving metals:

Reduction by dissolving metals e.g. zinc and acetic or hydrochloric acid, zinc and sodium hydroxide, zinc-copper couple and ethanol, etc. In this reaction, earlier ‘nascent’ hydrogen was considered to be reducing agent. Now it is believed that there is an electron-transfer from the metal to the substrate leading to the formation of carbanion, which is followed by the abstraction of a proton from the solvent. Thus, reduction with a zinc-ethanol couple may be formulated as

$\text{Zn}\to \text{Z}{{\text{n}}^{2+}}+2{{\text{e}}^{-}}$

$\text{RX}+{{\text{e}}^{-}}\to {{\text{X}}^{-}}+\text{R}\xrightarrow[{}]{{{\text{e}}^{-}}}\text{R}{{:}^{-}}$

${{\text{R}}^{-}}+{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{OH}\to \text{R}-\text{H}+{{\,}^{-}}\text{O}{{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}$

concepts.title: Using reducing agents like LiAlH4, NaBH4
concepts.conceptBody:
Reduction by reducing agents like $\mathbf{LiAl}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{4}}}\mathbf{,NaB}{{\mathbf{H}}_{\mathbf{4}}}$ etc.

Primary and secondary alkyl halides are readily reduced to alkanes by lithium aluminium hydride $\text{LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ while reduction of tertiary halides with $\text{LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ gives mainly alkenes. On the other hand, sodium borohydride $\text{NaB}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ reduces secondary and tertiary, but not primary, whereas triphenyltin hydride $\text{P}{{\text{h}}_{3}}\text{SnH}$ reduces all three types of alkyl halide. So each reducing agent is specific in its action.

(a) $4\text{R}-\text{X}+\text{LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\to 4\text{R}-\text{H}+\text{LiX}+\text{Al}{{\text{X}}_{3}}(\text{X}\ne \text{F})$

$\text{or}\,\text{ R}-\text{X}+\text{H}{{:}^{(-)}}\to \text{R}-\text{H}+{{\text{X}}^{-}}\left( {{\text{H}}^{-}}\text{comes from LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}} \right)$

(b) $\text{R}-\text{X}+{{\left( \text{n}-{{\text{C}}_{4}}{{\text{H}}_{9}} \right)}_{3}}\text{SnH}\to \text{R}-\text{H}+{{\left( \text{n}-{{\text{C}}_{4}}{{\text{H}}_{9}} \right)}_{3}}\text{SnX}$

$\text{LiAl}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ is not useful for 3 alkyl halide which is converted into alkenes. In such cases $\text{NaB}{{\text{H}}_{4}}$ or $\text{TPH}$ is used.

$1,2,3\text{ alkyl halide }\frac{\text{ TPH }}{(\text{ TPH }\to \text{ PhgSnH })}\text{ Alkane }$

concepts.title: Reduction of alcohols, carbonyl compounds and acid derivatives
concepts.conceptBody:
Reduction of alcohols, carbonyl compounds, acids and acid derivatives:

Alcohols, aldehydes, ketones, carboxylic acid their derivatives like acid halides and acid amides can be reduced by HI and red phosphorous to alkanes.

$\text{R}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{OH}+2\text{HI}\xrightarrow[{}]{\text{ red P}}\text{R}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+{{\text{I}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{R}-\text{CHO}+4\text{HI}\xrightarrow[{}]{\text{ red P}}\text{R}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+2{{\text{I}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{R}-\text{CO}-{{\text{R}}^{\prime }}+4\text{HI}\xrightarrow[{}]{\text{ red P}}\text{R}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{2}}+{{\text{R}}^{\prime }}\text{H}+2{{\text{I}}_{2}}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{R}-\text{COOH}+6\text{HI}\xrightarrow[{}]{\text{ red P}}\text{R}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+3{{\text{I}}_{2}}+2{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{R}-\text{COCl}+6\text{HI}\xrightarrow[{}]{\text{ red P}}\text{R}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+3{{\text{I}}_{2}}++\text{HCI}+{{\text{H}}_{2}}\text{O}$

$\text{R}-\text{CON}{{\text{H}}_{2}}+6\text{HI}\xrightarrow[{}]{\text{ red P}}\text{R}-\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}+3{{\text{I}}_{2}}+\text{N}{{\text{H}}_{4}}\text{OH}$

Reduction of esters with red phosphorus and HI gives two alkanes

We can use other reducing agents also reduce alkyl halide to alkanes.

$\text{R}-\text{X}\xrightarrow[{}]{\text{P}+\text{HI}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}$

or

$\text{Zn}+\text{HCl}$

or

$\text{Zn}+\text{C}{{\text{H}}_{3}}\text{OH}$

or

${{\text{C}}_{2}}{{\text{H}}_{5}}\text{OH}+\text{Na}$

Example 

Prepare

(i) Butane from chloro ethane using the Corey - House synthesis.

(ii) Deuteropropane from isopropyl bromide.

Solution:
